question.text: Match the correct hedging action for each scenario: (i) Exporter receiving EUR, (ii) Bond holder expecting rate rise, (iii) Portfolio manager fearing market crash.

answer.text: (i) Sell EURINR futures (ii) Sell bond futures (iii) Sell index futures
(i) Buy EURINR futures (ii) Buy bond futures (iii) Buy index futures
(i) Sell EURINR futures (ii) Buy bond futures (iii) Sell index futures
(i) Buy EURINR futures (ii) Sell bond futures (iii) Buy index futures

explanation.text: **The Logic:** (i) Exporter will receive EUR, risks EUR depreciation → sell EURINR futures. (ii) Bond holder with rising rates → bond prices fall → sell bond futures (go long on interest rate). (iii) Portfolio manager fears crash → sell index futures to offset portfolio losses. **The Trap:** For bond futures, the inverse price-yield relationship means selling futures = hedging against rising rates. Many confuse this with the direct relationship in currency and equity markets. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series I Ch5 (currency hedging), Series IV Ch5 (bond hedging), Series VIII Ch5 (equity hedging) each cover their respective hedge directions. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The common thread: all three are SHORT hedges — selling futures to protect against adverse price movements in their respective assets.
